data.title: Polymarket Traders Bet that Bitcoin Won't Top $138,000 This Year
data.description:
Bitcoin price predictions for 2025 are starting to cool. Despite the hype around a potential rally, market odds are showing a more conservative take. On Polymarket, traders are betting Bitcoin won't go higher than $138,000 this year, with some downside scenarios placing it as low as $59,000, according to data posted by user Ashwin on X. That's a far cry from the six-figure moonshots often thrown around in crypto circles.

Prediction Markets Keep Hype in Check

The Polymarket odds suggest a 60% upside from current levels, but it's hardly euphoric. "The $138K Bitcoin price target may not seem bullish to most Bitcoiners," Ashwin noted, explaining that sentiment has been weighed down by recent uncertainty, including fallout from U.S. tariffs under the Trump administration.

Historical Patterns Offer a Glimmer of Hope

Still, not everyone is downbeat. Economist Timothy Peterson posted on X that Bitcoin has a 75% chance of hitting new highs within nine months based on a decade of seasonality data. April and October tend to be strong months, with average returns of 13% and 22%. Meanwhile, CryptoQuant analysts say the $84K–$85K zone is a key level to watch—dip below it, and selling could accelerate fast.

At the time of writing, Bitcoin is sitting at $86,363. But between cautious prediction markets and historical tailwinds, traders are torn between bracing for impact—or loading up before the next leg higher.

data.author: Annika Masrani

data.title: Trump's Agenda Accidentally Helps Europe Crush U.S. Stocks
data.description:
Trump's foreign policy shake-ups are doing what years of sluggish growth couldn't—lighting a fire under European stocks. The STOXX Europe 600 is up 8% this year, outpacing the S&P 500 by over 10 percentage points. It's Trump's tough talk on alliances and tariffs that has jolted Europe into action, unleashing massive Fiscal stimulus, defense spending, and relaxed budget rules. Now, investors are finally treating the region like a serious opportunity.

Arkema Revs Up as Construction Spending Picks Up

French chemical maker Arkema (FR:AKE) is one of the quieter winners. Hit hard during the industry's downturn, the stock now looks primed for a rebound as European governments pour money into infrastructure. Portfolio manager Thomas Shrager told Barron's that Arkema should benefit from construction demand and recent acquisitions, calling the stock "cheap" after a stretch of underperformance.

Porsche Holds Strong Despite Auto Tariff Threats

Even as Trump threatens tariffs on European autos, Shrager still sees value in Porsche (DE:PAH3). "The guy who spends money on a Porsche doesn't care if it's $220,000 or $200,000," he said. Unlike mass-market rivals, Porsche's luxury buyers are less price sensitive—and its shares, trading at 14.5x earnings, still look reasonable.

BAT's Dividend Lures Value Hunters

Then there's British American Tobacco (GB:BATS). While slower than peers in going smoke-free, BAT offers a hefty 7.5% dividend yield and trades at just 8.8x earnings. It seems that U.S. valuations are stretched, and therefore income-focused investors are now turning to Europe—and BAT's payout makes it hard to ignore.

Investors can compare these stocks side by side using TipRanks' Stocks Comparison Tool. Among the three, Arkema (AKE) currently holds the most bullish analyst consensus with a Strong Buy rating. The average price target for AKE is €101.71, which implies an upside potential of 39.4%.

data.title: XRP Tanks Despite Ripple Beating the SEC
data.description:
XRP (XRP-USD) dropped 3.8% to $2.35 on March 27, even after Ripple officially ended its four-year battle with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Traders had expected a price surge after Ripple dropped its cross-appeal and got $75 million of its $125 million fine returned. But that legal win didn't move the needle, as the price continued to slide despite the news. Instead, XRP is flashing red across the board.

Ripple's Legal Win Fizzles in the Market

Despite Ripple's Chief Legal Officer Stuart Alderoty announcing the settlement and potential lifting of an SEC injunction, investors didn't bite. XRP's muted reaction suggests many had already priced in the news—or were hoping for more. That lack of excitement has triggered some profit-taking, leaving the price stuck near key support levels.

XRP’s Technical Signals Have Turned Bearish Fast

It's not just the headlines dragging XRP down. The token has confirmed a bearish flag breakdown, and according to Cointelegraph, XRP could fall to $1.60 if support at $2.22 fails. Meanwhile, open interest in XRP futures has dropped by 5% in 24 hours, and funding rates turned negative—two big red flags showing traders are leaning bearish.

At the time of writing, XRP is sitting at $2.36.

data.title: Nvidia's H20 AI Chips Face a Shortage in China, Says Chinese Partner H3C
data.description:
Semiconductor giant Nvidia's (NVDA) H20 artificial intelligence (AI) chips are facing a shortage in China amid increasing demand, according to Chinese partner H3C. The news was first reported by Reuters, citing reference to a client notice. Nvidia's H20 AI chips are among the few advanced AI processors legally permitted for sale in China. H3C warned that their shortage could pose a serious threat to China's AI ambitions.

H3C manufactures data center servers and is one of the OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) partners for the sale of Nvidia's chips in the mainland. Notably, H3C's H20 inventory is rapidly depleting, with new shipments expected only by mid-April. Owing to the limited supply, H3C has decided to distribute the chips on the basis of a profit-first approach, by prioritizing long-term customers who offer higher profit margins.

Nvidia's H20 AI Chips Grab Headlines

The H20 was launched in 2023, after the U.S. administration tightened the restrictions on chip exports in October 2023, fearing their misuse in China’s military warfare. Nvidia's H20 chips are designed specifically for export to China and are compliant with the U.S. export control requirements.

Following the launch of DeepSeek AI's low-cost R1 model in January, several Chinese companies are vying to adopt its technology, leading to a massive demand for the H20 chips. However, ongoing trade tensions and the related supply chain challenges are affecting their import into China. Large Chinese tech companies, including Alibaba (BABA), Tencent Holdings (TCEHY), and TikTok owner ByteDance have also ramped up orders of the H20 chips.

Meanwhile, recent reports suggest that Nvidia's H20 chip exports could be hurt by China's new energy standards. The National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) is pushing Chinese firms to use chips that comply with its new environmental rules, which the H20 chip currently does not meet, posing a potential threat to its exports.

Is NVDA Stock a Buy, Hold, or Sell?

On TipRanks, NVDA stock commands a Strong Buy consensus rating based on 39 Buys and three Hold ratings. Also, the average Nvidia price target of $176.54 implies 55.2% upside potential from current levels. In the past year, NVDA stock has gained 26.1%.

data.title: Trump's "Larger than Currently Planned" Tariffs Hit Dogecoin Gains and Slam XRP
data.description:
Trump's latest tariff threats are stirring up the crypto market again. In a Truth Social post, the U.S. president warned of "large scale Tariffs, far larger than currently planned" if the European Union and Canada act against the U.S. economy. The move rattled risk assets like Bitcoin, Ethereum (ETH-USD), and XRP (XRP-USD)—though Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) still managed a small gain before losing steam.

XRP Slides while Dogecoin Pops then Drops

XRP and Solana (SOL-USD) both dipped around 2% after Trump's comments, while Ethereum and BNB (BNB-USD) held steady. Dogecoin briefly climbed 3.5% before fading. Meanwhile, Sui Network's SUI (SUI-USD) surged 7% ahead of its Walrus Network launch. But overall, the market's mood turned cautious.

PCE Data and Asian Crypto Strength Offer Hope

Attention is turning to the March 28 PCE inflation data, which could sway Fed interest rate decisions and influence Bitcoin's next move. Traders also see a potential shift eastward. HashKey Capital's Jupiter Zheng told CoinDesk that "the next leg of the bull market may find its footing in Asia," where crypto regulation is more supportive. BTSE's Jeff Mei added that despite recent fear, "Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies have recovered… even as stock markets dropped."

Staying informed has never been more important. Tools like TipRanks' Cryptocurrency Center can help investors track performance, sentiment, and news around their favorite tokens—all in one place. Click on the image below to find out more.

data.title: Trump Slaps 25% Tariffs on Foreign Cars, Shakes Auto Market
data.description:
“Tariffs” is undoubtedly a prime candidate for the 2025 word of the year, as President Trump announced that the U.S. would impose a 25% tariff on all cars and car parts not made in the country starting April 3. The move aims to give American auto jobs and industry a competitive advantage, but it’s stirring strong reactions both at home and abroad.

New Tariffs on Top of Existing Ones

The tariff will apply to finished vehicles and parts alike. There had been talk about sparing auto parts, but that's off the table now, unless those parts meet strict U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade rules. Existing duties, like the 2.5% import tax and a long-standing 25% "chicken tax" on light trucks, will still apply, meaning this new tariff stacks on top. Cars and trucks made in Canada and Mexico will also be hit, despite free trade deals, because of unrelated disputes, like fentanyl trafficking.

Unsurprisingly, world leaders aren't pleased. Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney called the move "a direct attack on Canadian workers" and hinted at payback. The European Union also voiced regret, saying it would protect its economic interests. Meanwhile, Trump doubled down, warning he'd slap even bigger tariffs on countries that retaliate or “hurt the U.S. economy.”

Industry Implications

Automakers and industry groups are also worried. Nearly half of all new cars sold in the U.S. in 2024 were made outside the country. Mexico is the top source, followed by Japan, Canada, and South Korea. Popular models like the Toyota RAV4 (TM), Honda Civic (HMC), and GM's Silverado (GM) come from plants in Canada or Mexico. Big-name brands like BMW (EXTRA:BMW) , Audi, and Hyundai (HYMLF) also ship large volumes.

The tariffs could hit these companies hard. Analysts at Cox Automotive estimate the added cost per vehicle imported from Canada or Mexico could be around $6,000 – a sharp spike that could make many cars less affordable for U.S. buyers. Japanese and South Korean vehicles could increase costs from $2,000 to $3,000. With nearly 50% of U.S. car sales relying on imports, a big chunk of the market is affected.

Carmakers may try to shift production to U.S. plants, but that's a long and pricey move. Many are bracing for tighter supply, slower production, and fewer sales.

Stocks' Reaction

Of course, Wall Street noticed. Since the announcement, stocks for big U.S. automakers have taken a hit. General Motors dropped 6.5% after hours. Stellantis (STLA), which makes Jeep and Ram, fell 2.5%, while Ford’s (F) needle hardly moved.  Tesla (TSLA), which builds all its U.S. cars at home, rose around 1%, likely because it's not affected by the new rules.

The bottom line? Prices are likely going up. With fewer cars on lots and higher sticker prices, car buyers and sellers alike will feel the heat. The ripple effects could stretch far beyond the auto industry as supply chains shift and trade tensions grow.

We’ve assembled all the auto manufacturers mentioned in the article and would potentially be affected by the new tariff policy. Tipranks’ comparison tool can provide a comprehensive perspective on each company and the industry as a whole.

data.title: Stock Market News Today, 3/27/25 – Futures Down as Trump's Auto Tariffs Trigger Uncertainty
data.description:
U.S. stock futures were down early Thursday as investors assessed the impact of President Trump’s announcement of a 25% tariff on cars made outside the U.S. Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA), and the S&P 500 (SPX) were down 0.13%, 0.06%, and 0.09%, respectively, at 4:56 a.m. EST, March 27.

The tariff announcement hurt investor sentiment, causing General Motors (GM) and Ford (F) stocks to drop about 6% and 5%, respectively, in the extended trading session.

The market uncertainty, fueled by concerns about tariff impacts and possible retaliation from affected nations, has caused volatility and raised fears of wider economic effects.

Wednesday’s regular trading session saw a widespread sell-off, particularly in the tech sector. The S&P 500, the Nasdaq Composite (NDAQ), and the Dow Jones fell by 1.12%, 2.04%, and 0.31%, respectively.

This downturn ended a three-day winning streak, which had followed a month-long selloff driven by concerns over tariffs and the U.S. economy’s outlook.

Meanwhile, investors remain focused on upcoming economic data reports as recession fears loom. Today, the second estimate of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) for the fourth quarter will be released. Also, Advanced Wholesale and Retail Inventories data, Pending Home Sales report, and Initial Jobless Claims for the week ended March 22, will be made public today.

Further, today's earnings calendar remains light, with companies such as Winnebago (WGO), Lululemon Athletica (LULU), and TD Synnex (SNX) reporting results today.

At the time of writing, the U.S. 10-year treasury yield was up, floating near 4.367%. Simultaneously, WTI crude oil futures are trending lower, hovering near $69.40 per barrel as of the last check.

Elsewhere, European indices opened sharply lower as the auto sector remained under pressure from Trump's 25% tariff announcement on non-American cars, set to take effect from April 2.

Asia-Pacific Markets Ended Higher on Thursday

Asia-Pacific indices were mixed today as investors reacted to President Trump’s announcement of a 25% tariff on car imports.

At the same time, Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index was up 0.41%. Further, China's Shanghai Composite and Shenzhen Component indices gained 0.15% and 0.23%, respectively. Also, Japan's Topix index was up 0.09%, while the Nikkei index was down 0.6%.

data.title: KB Home's Stock (KBH) Hits 52-Week Low on Earnings and Revenue Stumble
data.description:
KB Home (KBH) reported disappointing fiscal first-quarter results, missing both earnings and revenue estimates, and subsequently revised its fiscal 2025 revenue guidance downwards. The company’s shares dropped 5.6%, marking their lowest point in 52 weeks. Factors such as macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties, coupled with affordability concerns, have slowed homebuying demand, according to CEO Jeffrey Mezger. Consequently, KB Home has reduced its revenue targets, reflecting a dip in net orders. Despite a rise in the average selling price, the number of homes delivered fell by 9%, impacting operating income and margins. Analyst reactions were mixed, with some adjusting price targets downwards due to persisting market challenges.

Top and Bottom-line Misses

KB Home is one of the leading homebuilding companies in the U.S., focusing on the construction and sale of new homes. The company recently reported a decline in revenue and earnings for the quarter ended February 28, 2025, with revenue of $1.39 billion, a 5% decrease from the previous year. The company’s operating income decreased to $127.3 million, with a narrower operating income margin of 9.2%, down from 10.8%, primarily due to lower housing gross profit margins. Net income also decreased by 21% to $109.6 million, as reflected in the diluted earnings per share, which fell by 15% to $1.49, missing expectations by $0.10.

The financial services segment experienced a 35% decline in pretax income, primarily due to a decrease in equity income from the company’s mortgage banking joint venture, which was offset by a reduction in home deliveries and loan originations. From a balance sheet perspective, inventory increased by 7% to $5.94 billion, and investments in land and development grew by 57%. Despite these increases, net orders declined by 17%, negatively affecting the ending backlog of homes and values, although the community count grew by 7%. The company’s debt-to-capital ratio slightly increased to 30.5%.

Looking towards the full year of 2025, KB Home projects housing revenues between $6.60 billion and $7.00 billion, with an average selling price of $480,000 to $495,000. The guidance anticipates a housing gross profit margin of 19.2% to 20.0%.

Analysts Remain Cautious

Analysts following the company have taken a cautious stance on the stock. For instance, BTIG analyst Carl Reichardt has reiterated a neutral stance on the shares, noting that the company’s financial performance for the first quarter of 2025 did not meet expectations, with lower-than-anticipated earnings per share driven by reduced delivery volumes and increased selling, general, and administrative expenses. The demand for KB Home’s products has declined, resulting in a reduction in the company's guidance for fiscal year 2025.

Meanwhile, Keefe Bruyette's Jade Rahmani has also adjusted their outlook, lowering the price target for KB Home to $65 (from $76) while maintaining a Market Perform rating, noting that near-term risks are balanced between potential stabilization in the new home market and the likelihood of further deterioration.

KB Home is rated a Hold overall, based on the recent recommendations of 13 analysts. The average price target for KBH stock is $69.42, which represents a potential upside of 17.44% from current levels.

data.title: M&A News: Nvidia (NVDA) Nears Deal to Buy Server Rental Startup Lepton AI
data.description:
Semiconductor giant Nvidia (NVDA) is nearing a deal to acquire data server rental player Lepton AI, according to The Information. The exact details of the deal are currently unknown, but the report indicates that the acquisition could be valued at several hundred million dollars, citing people familiar with the matter. If the deal goes through, Nvidia's acquisition of Lepton AI would mark its entry into the rapidly-growing cloud and enterprise software market. Some of the major players in this space include large cloud providers such as Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOGL).

Lepton AI is a newly founded GPU (Graphic Processing Unit) reseller, established in 2023. The company specializes in renting out data center servers powered by Nvidia's artificial intelligence (AI) chips. Notably, Lepton AI raised $11 million in a seed round from investors CRV and Fusion Fund. It competes with a relatively larger player, Together AI, which has raised over $500 million from venture capital investors since its inception in 2022.

Nvidia Strenthens its AI Ecosystem with Strategic Acquisitions

Nvidia is strengthening its broader AI ecosystem through strategic acquisitions, such as the recently announced purchase of synthetic data startup Gretel. Gretel has developed a platform that allows developers to train their AI models on synthetic data with enhanced security and privacy aspects. Meanwhile, Lepton AI offers high-performance computing hardware and cloud-native software solutions that support both AI training and inferencing.

These acquisitions are enabling Nvidia to deliver end-to-end AI solutions, from hardware to cloud-based services, thus fortifying its competitive position in the AI industry. Additionally, they represent a strategic move by Nvidia to expand beyond its core semiconductor business. Overall, Nvidia is making targeted acquisitions to effectively compete in the multi-billion-dollar hyperscaler data center market.

Is NVDA a Good Stock to Buy Now?

Despite the current downturn in the stock market and ongoing trade war, Wall Street remains highly bullish about Nvidia's stock trajectory. Owing to these challenges, NVDA stock has dropped 15.3% so far this year, dragging down its yearly gain to 26.1%.

On TipRanks, NVDA stock has a Strong Buy consensus rating based on 39 Buys and three Hold ratings. Also, the average Nvidia price target of $176.54 implies 55.2% upside potential from current levels.

data.title: OpenAI Is Set to Close $40B SoftBank-Led Funding at $300B Valuation
data.description:
OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, is close to finalizing a $40 billion funding round led by SoftBank (SFTBY), according to Bloomberg. This deal would push OpenAI's valuation to $300 billion, up from $157 billion. If completed, it would rank among the largest private company investments ever, according to PitchBook.

As part of the deal, SoftBank plans to invest $7.5 billion initially, alongside $2.5 billion from a syndicate of investors, according to sources familiar with the talks. A second round of $30 billion is expected later this year, with SoftBank contributing $22.5 billion and another $7.5 billion coming from the syndicate.

If the deal goes through successfully, SoftBank could surpass Microsoft (MSFT) as OpenAI's top investor, deepening the connection between OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and SoftBank's Masayoshi Son.

Heavyweights Back OpenAI's AI Push

SoftBank isn't the only one betting big on OpenAI. The funding round is expected to include heavyweights like Magnetar Capital, Coatue Management, Founders Fund, and Altimeter Capital.

Reportedly, Illinois-based hedge fund Magnetar Capital alone is eyeing a $1 billion investment, showing strong confidence in OpenAI's future.

OpenAI Eyes Massive Revenue Growth

At the same time, OpenAI is gearing up for significant revenue expansion. The company expects to triple its revenue this year to $12.7 billion, according to a Bloomberg report. The company reported $3.7 billion in revenue for 2024, largely driven by its expanding subscription services for both individuals and businesses.

By 2026, OpenAI aims to hit nearly $30 billion in annual revenue.

SoftBank Doubles Down on AI

SoftBank has been aggressively expanding in AI. Earlier this month, it announced plans to borrow $16 billion for AI investments, with an option for another $8 billion in 2026. It has also committed $15 billion to Stargate, a joint venture with OpenAI and Oracle (ORCL) aimed at strengthening U.S. AI infrastructure.

With this latest investment in OpenAI, SoftBank is cementing its role as a dominant force in the AI race.

Who Is the Biggest Investor in OpenAI?

So far, Microsoft and SoftBank have emerged as the two largest investors in OpenAI. We used the TipRanks Stock Comparison tool to compare their performance. Investors who seek to gain exposure to OpenAI can do so by investing in one of these companies after thorough research.
